**Chapter 1 – Red Text**  

00:03 am – three blood‑drop emojis flash on my screen, like a warning sign from somewhere I never asked for. I stare at the glow of the phone’s backlight, the cheap plastic edges cutting into the skin of my thumb.  

*Bloody hell, three blood‑drops on my screen. If this isn’t a sign that the school’s Wi‑Fi is haunted, I’m buying a vampire‑repellent for tomorrow’s exam.*  

I flip the phone over, thumb grazing the tiny glass, and the emojis wobble a little, as if they were trying to crawl out of the screen. I open the chat window, hoping the sender left a name or a message, but the thread is empty – just the three little drops sitting there, like they’re waiting for me to type something back. I tap the screen again; the icons pulse once, twice, then settle into the same dead‑silence as a moth on a wall. No sender, no timestamp, nothing. It’s just… there.  

I mutter under my breath, “You’ve got to be kidding me.” The hallway outside my dormitory is quiet, the rain pattering against the lead‑lined windows, a thin mist seeping through the gaps in the stonework. The old building seems to breathe in and out with the storm, the kind of rhythm that makes you think the walls themselves have a pulse. My room is a cramped space—narrow bed, a single chest of drawers, a lamp that flickers whenever I’m trying to stay up past curfew. I pull the notebook from my pocket, its navy‑blue cover already bent at the corners, and start to write.

*Entry 1/8 – “Red Text”*

> 00:03 am – three blood emojis arrive. No sender. No explanation. 
> I’m not a vampire, but I feel like a teenage detective in a bad‑cop movie. My dad always says that “the world is full of red herrings” – I’m starting to think he’s right. 
> The cat‑cat‑cat (Molly) hasn’t shown up yet, but I can already hear the echo of a purr in my head, as if she’s waiting for me to spill something first. 
> I’ll have to check the Wi‑Fi logs later. If this is a prank, someone’s got a very good sense of timing. If it’s a warning, well… maybe I should start carrying garlic?  
> (I’m laughing at myself now, but the anxiety is still there. The rain sounds louder than ever.)

I sigh, a little huff of breath that makes the cheap wood of my desk creak. The ceiling fan above me whirs lazily, trying to push the damp air around. I glance at the clock on the wall—digital, blinking 00:05—its numbers flashing like a metronome for a secret dance I’m supposed to join. My dad’s voice pops into my head, a crackly reminder from a recent video call he sent before his next assignment to the Middle East: “Keep your head above the line, son. The world’s a mess of headlines and blood.” I think about that line now, and my throat tightens just enough to make my heart feel like it’s beating in a tin drum.

The next thing I need to do is tell someone. I can’t sit on this alone; the night has a habit of magnifying every half‑finished thought into a full‑blown conspiracy. I shuffle to the door, the old brass knob cold under my fingertips, and push it open just enough to peek into the hallway. The corridor is a thin strip of dim light, the echo of footsteps muffled by the rain‑softened stone. I see Mika, a classmate from the maths set, leaning against the lock‑up with his phone held out like a torch.

“Yo,” I whisper, “Did you get anything weird on your phone last night?”

Mika raises an eyebrow, the glint of his glasses catching the hallway’s amber bulb. “Not really,” he says, “but I think the Wi‑Fi’s acting up. My sister says the network’s been glitchy since the new router. Maybe it’s just a… glitch?”

He shrugs, and I hear the faint rustle of his shirt as he steps past. I’m about to close the door when I catch a whisper from the corner of the hallway. The caretaker, Mr. Hargrave, is shuffling a bag of cleaning supplies past the stairwell. He mutters something under his breath, the words barely audible over the rain.

“Bloody hell,” he says, more to himself than anyone else.

The sound reverberates off the stone walls, a low, gruff chuckle that feels like an echo of the emojis themselves. I pause, notebook still open, and stare at the three drops on the screen, feeling like the caretaker’s grumble is a tiny affirmation that something is indeed out of order.

I close the door, lock it with a soft click, and sink back into my bed. The mattress sags under my weight, the springs groaning as they remember the weight of dozens of other boys who’ve tossed and turned in the same room over the years. I pull the red lipstick out of my pocket—just a dab of cheap bright colour, something I sometimes use to “stain” my skin when I want to look more dramatic for a photo— and smear a tiny line on the back of my hand, a half‑hearted imitation of blood. It looks ridiculous, but it feels like a ritual, a way to claim the night for myself.

*I’m going to write this down. If anyone reads it later, I’ll have proof that I wasn’t just a prank victim.*  

I type, more slowly now, trying to capture the feel of the moment rather than just the facts.  

> “At 00:03 am, the phone buzzed like a cheap alarm clock. Three blood emojis, no sender, no context. I’m lying on a thin mattress, listening to the rain on the stone, and the caretaker mutters “bloody hell”. My red lipstick is already a stain on my skin, as if the night wants to colour me in. I don’t know who sent it, but I think it’s a sign. Maybe the club that’s been whispering about “Crimson” is finally showing up.”

My thoughts spin. I’ve heard rumors about a secret society called the Crimson Club. Old Year‑book photos show a handful of kids in crimson scarves, a few who are *supposed* to be “dark‑humor” enthusiasts. A senior once said, “If you’re looking for drama, the Crimson Club is where the blood runs thin.” The phrase stuck in my mind like a half‑sung lyric. Now, three blood emojis appear. The club’s name suddenly feels like a billboard in the night sky.

I glance at the clock again. The digital numbers have turned 00:05, and the phone’s vibrations pulse a faint reminder: a tiny, almost imperceptible buzz, like a heartbeat trying to sneak through a wall. I stare at it, then at the red lipstick smear on my hand, and feel a stupid, teenage certainty that something’s about to happen—maybe a meeting, maybe a prank, maybe a real danger.

I decide to log out of the school’s Wi‑Fi network later and run a quick scan. I’ll see if any hidden messages are tucked in the router logs, maybe a sign that the emojis were a broadcast from a device somewhere in the old wing. For now, I’ll write a few more lines, let the ink settle, and then try to fall asleep. The rain has turned the windows into a sheet of water, and the candle I keep in my room flickers dimly, casting a soft orange glow on the notebook pages.

> “The rain is making the stone walls sound like they’re humming a lullaby. I can almost hear the chapel’s organ playing in the background, though it’s silent. If I close my eyes, I might hear the distant echo of a chant… but I’m probably just tired. I’ll sleep on it. Tomorrow’s maths test, a blood‑donation drive, and an essay about “The Role of Blood in Shakespeare”. My life is a mess of red— and maybe, just maybe, this mess has a shape.”

I set the phone down, the screen still glowing with the three drops, and let the darkness swallow the room. I pull the blanket tighter, feel the cotton’s weight against my shoulders, and listen to the rhythm of the rain. In the back of my mind, a tiny voice whispers, “You’re not a vampire, Jammie. You’re a kid who’s gotten a text from the dark side of the internet.”

The night presses in, but I’m ready—or at least, I think I am. I’ll keep an eye on the emojis, the caretaker’s mutter, and whatever strange secret society thinks it can draw a line of crimson across my teenage existence.  

And then, barely five minutes later, the phone buzzes again. This time it’s a single 👀 emoji, sitting on the screen like an eyeball staring back at me. I freeze, notebook mid‑sentence, and a tiny part of me wonders whether someone else is watching this whole thing unfold.  

I close the diary for now, press the phone to my chest, and let the rain fall. The emojis have taken the night hostage, and I, a sleep‑deprived scholarship boy with a red‑stained hand, am the only one who knows what they’re trying to say.  

*End of entry.*
step_outputs.7:
**Chapter 2 – The Circle of Red**  

00:07 am – after the three blood‑drop emojis vanished, the phone stayed dead, as if it were waiting for a reply. I turned it off, slipped it into the pocket of my navy‑blue Moleskine, and crawled back into the cramped mattress. The rain kept hammering the stone walls, each drip sounding like a tiny alarm clock I could never turn off. My red‑lipstick‑stained hand lay against the night‑stand, the smear looking more like a warning than a joke. I tried to drift back to sleep, but the night buzzed with the same three drops in my mind, looping over and over. When the alarm finally rang at 7 am, I was already half‑in‑the‑dark, the candle in the corner sputtering like an exhausted firefly.  

The school’s bell—an old iron chime that always seems to echo a little longer than it should—cut through the morning mist. I swung my legs out of the covers, pulled on my blazer (the cuffs still rolled up from the night before), and headed down the dim corridor toward the common room where the first‑year scholars gathered for a steaming pot of Earl Grey. The hallway smelled of damp wool and pine cleaner, and the lockers that line each side were a patchwork of bright colours and dull metal. One of the lockers—Number 23, the one I always use for my maths notes—had a thin, glossy smear of red at the bottom. It wasn’t a splash, just a single line, like a drop of ink had fallen and dried before anyone noticed. I froze, notebook in hand, the pocket‑watch of my wrist ticking louder than usual.  

*Entry 2/8 – “Red Locker”*  

> 07:12 am – thin red smear on locker 23. No explanation. My gut says “sign”. I’m not a paranoid kid, but this feels… *bloody*. I’m still wearing the red lipstick on my hand; it’s a reminder that I can make a mess if I want.  
> I text Zara. I’ve got a Discord channel that’s just emojis. I’m hoping she can decode it before I accidentally turn the whole school into a vampire‑hunt.  

I opened the app on my phone, fingers shaking a little, and typed a quick message to Zara Patel, the tech‑savvy girl from my robotics team.  

> **Jammie:** Hey, you seen any weird Discord channels lately? Something called #CrimsonClub maybe?  

The reply pinged back within seconds.  

> **Zara:** Oh, that’s the one. I dug it up yesterday—just a chat with a bunch of blood emojis and cryptic lines. I’m thinking it’s a prank, but the emojis are weirdly consistent. I’ll send you the invite code. Also, my smartwatch buzzed at 00:03 am—same time as your emojis. Could be synced.  

She sent the invite link. I tapped it, the Discord window opening like a dark theatre curtain. The server was a single channel titled **#CrimsonClub**, and the first few messages were pure emojis: 🩸🩸🩸, then a series of tiny symbols that looked like puzzle pieces—✂️, 🎭, 📜— interspersed with a single line of text in a faint font that read, “If you see this, you’re in.” No usernames, no avatars. The channel was locked, with a rule: *Only blood emojis allowed.* I stared at the screen, the neon glow of the red emojis reflecting off my eyes like cheap neon signs in a club bathroom.  

*I’m not a vampire, but I’m getting the impression that someone’s trying to draw a blood circle around me.*  

I tried to type a reply, but the bot only accepted emojis. I chose a single 🩸, hoping it would trigger something. The next message arrived instantly: a fresh blood‑emoji, then a tiny “fire‑alarm” symbol that looked like a 🔥—the only other emoji allowed in the chat. A voice, distorted and barely audible, crackled through the speaker of the Discord app.  

> **Unknown:** Welcome, child. The night is longer than the day.  

The words vanished as quickly as they appeared, leaving me with a lingering echo that felt like a secret being whispered in a hallway. I shut the app, turned off the phone, and slipped the invite code into my pocket‑notebook, scribbling a quick note: **CrimsonClub – invite: crimson123**.  

As I reached the common room, the morning announcement crackled over the intercom. Mrs. Pennington, the deputy head, announced that “the Crimson Club will be hosting a charity blood‑donation drive this term, encouraging all students to participate in the upcoming ‘Blood Night’ ceremony at the chapel on Friday evening.” The words hung in the air like an announcement of a prom, but with a twist of blood. My stomach twisted.  

> **Mrs. Pennington (over intercom):** “Students, please note that the Crimson Club will be visible from the 2nd floor corridor after lights out on Friday. Attendance is not required, but we encourage you to support the blood‑donation campaign.”  

She added, “Please remind anyone who receives strange emojis to report them to the pastoral care office. This will help keep our community safe.” The tone was dead‑pan, as if she were delivering a weather forecast, but the mention of “strange emojis” made the whole thing sound like a school‑wide prank gone wrong. I glanced around; most heads were buried in textbooks, the old professor’s “right as rain” jokes ringing out in the background.  

I joined the tea line, the hot water hissing as the kettle boiled. The school’s headmistress, Mrs. Finch, stood at the end of the line, her tweed blazer perfectly buttoned, her pocket‑watch ticking loudly as she sipped her Earl Grey. She gave me a smile, the kind that says “I see you, but I’m not going to ask about it.” I nodded politely, but my mind kept looping back to the red smear on locker 23.  

> **Mrs. Finch (softly):** “Jammie, you look like you’ve been up all night. Remember, the school’s policy is to keep the chapel’s lights on during curfew. No need for extra drama, lad.”  

I smiled, but the words felt like a thin veil over something darker. I remembered the caretaker, Mr. Hargrave, who had muttered “bloody hell” yesterday. I thought about the cat—Molly—who had kept a low‑profile through the night but now seemed to lurk near the locker doors, a soft purr audible whenever the locker’s door opened.  

I decided to investigate the locker further. I grabbed a small rag from the cleaning closet and dabbed at the red line. The smear turned out to be a fresh coat of strawberry jam, the kind we keep in the kitchen for toast. The custodian’s mop bucket sat empty, the water gone. The janitor, a stooped man with a bald head and a deep, gravelly voice, shuffled past the lockers with a bag of cleaning supplies. He glanced at my rag, raised an eyebrow, and muttered—  

> **Mr. Hargrave:** “Bloody typical.”  

His comment floated like a ghost in the hallway, and I felt a chill of recognition. “Typical” sounded like an admission that something was wrong, something he’d seen before, something he wouldn’t talk about.  

I slipped back to my room, notebook now heavier with the invite code and a fresh smear of strawberry jam on my fingertips. I opened a fresh page, the ink still wet, and wrote a quick paragraph about the locker, the janitor’s mutter, and the morning announcement.  

> “The janitor muttered ‘bloody typical’ as if the red line on locker 23 was a ritual, not a spill. I think someone’s trying to mark the school with blood, and I’m not sure whether it’s a joke or a warning. Zara’s Discord hint that the emojis are linked to a secret group makes it feel like a game, but the morning news about a ‘Blood Night’ ceremony feels like a real invitation. I’ll have to decide whether to investigate the Crimson Club or to keep my nose out of the mess.”  

I stared at the page, feeling the weight of the words, then glanced at the three blood emojis still flickering on my phone’s home screen. The phone buzzed again—a single 👀 emoji—just as I was about to shut it down. I paused, the candle’s flame trembling in the wind from the open window, and the night seemed to press in on me, as if it wanted a part of the story too.  

In the hallway, a faint laugh echoed from the opposite end of the corridor—Zara, I could hear her voice through the thin walls, half‑laugh, half‑worried.  

> **Zara (voice over the phone, after I’d texted her):** “I’m on it. I’ll try to see if we can pull a ‘blood‑code’ from the Discord messages. If it’s a real thing, we’ll get the council involved. If it’s a prank, we’ll make sure it doesn’t get anyone into trouble. Either way, we’ll keep our mouths shut about the emojis until we know what’s going on.”  

She paused, then added, “And Jammie, if you see any more blood on the walls, don’t touch it. I’m not going to be the one cleaning up a mess of ketchup and cat fur.”  

I laughed, a short, sharp bark that sounded more like a cough. The sound broke the silence, and I felt the weight of the moment lift just a little. I closed my eyes, breathed in the damp, pine‑scented air, and imagined the school’s corridors as a stage for a dark‑comic play—one where the script is written in emojis, the actors are teenagers, and the director is a caretaker muttering “bloody typical” under his breath.  

I didn’t have a plan, just a notebook and a red lipstick stain on my hand, and a growing suspicion that the Crimson Club wasn’t a joke at all, but a secret society that had already seeped into the school’s fabric, one drop at a time.  

*End of entry.*  

The rain drummed against the window, each tap a reminder that the night was still listening. I slipped my phone back into my pocket, set the notebook on the desk, and turned the kettle on for another cup of tea—this time, I let the steam linger, watching it rise like a tiny red mist, wondering if the next emoji would be another drop or a different symbol altogether.
step_outputs.8:
**Chapter 3 – Ghost‑Hunter Entrance**  

08:45 am – the alarm bell on the school’s old brass clock chimed twice, a thin “ding‑ding” that always feels like a reminder that the day is already halfway gone. I slipped my navy‑blue Moleskine into the pocket of my blazer, the pocket‑watch dangling from a chain that clicked against the leather every time I moved. The scar on my right knuckle, a souvenir from a previous “prank‑water‑bomb” explosion, throbbed faintly as I brushed the hallway’s wet tiles.  

The school’s “ghost‑hunt” legend is something you hear in the common room when the older boys are bored. Supposedly, a couple of years ago a group of first‑years claimed they heard whispers in the chapel after lights‑out, and the staff never proved it – they just replaced the old pipe organ with a digital synthesizer and the story died out. I had never believed the tales, but tonight, after the three blood‑drop emojis, the ghost‑hunt seemed a perfect excuse to get a look at the hidden library.  

I decided to put on a disguise: a cheap flashlight tucked into my jacket pocket, a rag‑wrapped notebook marked “Ghost‑Hunter’s Log”, and a half‑finished costume piece I’d seen on a Pinterest board – a black hoodie with a red‑tinted half‑mask that I’d bought for the school’s Halloween party. The mask felt ridiculous, but I’d rather look like a lanky amateur paranormal investigator than a curious teen with a phone full of mysterious emojis.  

The hallway smelled of damp wool and pine cleaner, the kind that never truly fades after a night of rain. I padded past the lockers, each one a potential clue. Locker 23 still bore the faint smear of strawberry jam, now slightly smeared across the metal like a secret ink‑stain. I brushed my fingertips over it, feeling the sticky residue, and whispered to myself, “If there’s a secret, it’s probably left a trace somewhere.”  

At the end of the corridor, a heavy oak door bore the faded brass plaque “Old Library – Restricted Access”. The sign above it read “Do Not Enter After 22:00 – Security”. My flashlight’s beam flickered against the dust‑covered glass, revealing a row of ancient tomes bound in leather, their spines cracked and yellowed. A thin crack in the floorboards suggested a hidden tunnel – the one the caretaker, Mr. Hargrave, had muttered about in his “bloody typical” comment yesterday.  

I pressed the door with my left hand, feeling the cold metal against my palm, and listened. No echo, just the distant hum of the school’s ventilation system. I slipped a hand into the pocket where I kept the pocket‑watch and, for a moment, imagined a countdown. The watch ticked, a steady “tick‑tick‑tick” that sounded like a metronome for a secret ceremony.  

*Entry 3/8 – “Ghost‑Hunter Entrance”*  

> 09:02 am – I’m in the old library, flashlight in hand, half‑mask on my face. I’m pretending to investigate ghosts because I want to see the Crimson Club’s meeting. The caretaker muttered “bloody typical” and the janitor’s bag of supplies is still missing. The locker smear is strawberry jam, not blood.  
> Lord Crimson’s first line was “Thou shalt pour thy sanguine tears upon the stone.” I’m already terrified, but I can’t deny the absurdity.  

A low, almost theatrical voice floated from the far corner of the room, a voice that seemed to belong to a stage director rehearsing a soliloquy. I turned the flashlight toward the source – a tall figure perched on a wooden stool, his hands resting on a battered table. The lamp above his head cast a reddish glow, highlighting the faded crimson pocket‑square pinned to his chest. He wore a navy‑blue blazer, a burgundy silk scarf, and a walking cane with a skull‑engraved handle that tapped rhythmically against the floor.  

“Ah, the night‑watch,” he said, his accent a blend of Received Pronunciation and a theatrical drawl, “I’ve been expecting you. The Crimson Club does not welcome casual passersby.” He gestured to a whiteboard on the opposite wall, where he’d hastily sketched a diagram in red ink: a circle labelled “Blood Night”, arrows pointing to “Chapel – 22:00”, a small box titled “Props – Ketchup, Fake Blood, Cat (Molly)”. Beneath it, a line of text in a bold font read, “If you see this, you’re in.”  

His eyes were a shade of amber that seemed to absorb the dim light, and his smile was half‑grin, half‑grimace. “Thomas Whitaker,” he announced, a flourish of his cane, “Lord Crimson, the orchestrator of the Crimson Club. I am here to ensure the blood of this school remains… flavorful.”  

I swallowed, feeling the dry cough of a nervous teen. “I’m not looking for blood, I’m looking for… a ghost,” I replied, trying to keep my voice steady. The flashlight trembled in my hand, the beam wobbling like a nervous dog. “I’m just a scholarship boy trying to get into the library after lights‑out. The… …the emojis on my phone… I’m trying to figure out what they mean.”  

He chuckled, a sound that rolled off his tongue like a theatrical whisper. “You are not the first to receive the signal. The Crimson Club is a satirical secret society. We are a group of students who, in the face of a world that treats us like automatons, decide to dress up the mundane with a splash of… *blood*.” He tapped the whiteboard, the red ink smearing a little as his fingertip pressed. “The purpose is simple: to remind the school that there are moments when the ordinary must be… *exaggerated*.”  

He lifted his hand, revealing a single drop of red ink on his fingertip – not blood, but a dab of cheap stage‑makeup. “Consider this a recruitment token,” he said, “If you accept, you will become a participant in the ritual, a storyteller of the night, a *witness* to the absurd.” He looked directly at me, his gaze piercing. “Or you can walk away, keep your notebook empty, and pretend that the world is as bland as the school’s prospectus.”  

I glanced at the whiteboard, at the tiny sketch of a cat’s paw beside the “Props” box. The figure’s outline resembled the stray ginger tabby, Molly, who I’d seen that morning, her scarred ear glinting in the rain‑slick hallway. The cat’s presence felt oddly comforting – a reminder that the “blood” we spoke of could be just a smear of ketchup or a real feline with a wounded paw.  

“Lord Crimson,” I muttered, the words tasting like iron on my tongue, “You’re… you’re being absurd, but you’re also… making sense.” I felt the red lipstick on my hand smudge a little, a faint line seeping into the paper of the notebook. “I’m not a vampire, I’m not a prankster. I’m just… curious.”  

He leaned forward, his cane clicking against the floorboards. “Curiosity is the blood of the club,” he said, the phrase dripping like a punchline. “But remember, the *real* blood is the charity drive. We will host a *Blood Night* in the chapel, a fake‑blood ceremony to raise funds for the local blood‑bank. The world will see us as theatrical delinquents, but the money will go to a real cause.” He paused, eyes flicking to the hallway door, then back to me. “If you stay, you will learn the art of performance. If you leave, you will remain a spectator to the joke.”  

I swallowed, the faint taste of the school’s tea lingering on my tongue, and thought of Mrs. Finch’s stern words from yesterday’s morning announcement: “No extra drama after curfew.” The club’s plan seemed to flirt with that rule, but in a way that made the drama *look* like a charity. I thought of Zara’s message about the Discord channel and the unknown chat‑bot that had replied with “Welcome, child.” The night was already pressing against the windows, rain pouring like a sheet of glass. The wind chime hanging from the chapel’s roof swayed lazily, its metallic clink echoing like a distant laugh.  

“Fine,” I said, my voice cracking slightly. “I’ll stay.” I felt a strange mix of relief and dread, like the moment you finally agree to go on a roller‑coaster after weeks of watching from the ground. “But I need to know… what do we actually do?”  

He smiled, a thin line that seemed to cut across his cheek. “First, you will wear the Crimson Club insignia – a small red button on your jacket. You will assist in gathering props. You will keep a diary, because the club believes that every *blood* story needs a *ink* companion. And you will—” He paused, eyes narrowing, “—you will watch the cat, Molly, as she becomes the *prop* for the blood‑tea toast. It’s all part of the satire.”  

The cat’s faint meow drifted from the corner of the room, where a small cardboard box lay open, a half‑full bowl of ketchup next to a torn piece of paper that read “Blood Donation – 5 pm”. The janitor’s bag of cleaning supplies stood empty, the mop bucket clean, the broom leaning against the wall. The red‑stained rag I had used to clean locker 23 lay abandoned on the floor, its juice now a darker, more realistic smear.  

I slipped the rag into my pocket, feeling the sticky residue against my palm. The stain was more than a joke; it felt like a token, a promise that something *real* might be waiting for me. I pressed the red button onto my blazer, the soft click sounding like a tiny gunfire. The button’s edge was sharp, like a blade, and I imagined the night becoming a stage where every line of dialogue was punctuated by a flicker of red.  

Lord Crimson stood, his cane tapping a rhythm against the floor. “Tonight, the Crimson Club meets in the chapel at 22:00. You will be the observer. Write, Jammie. Write.” He extended his hand, his fingers brushing against the red button on my jacket. I shook it, feeling the slight tremor of his grip, a mixture of authority and mockery.  

*I’m not a vampire, I’m not a detective. I’m a scholarship boy with a red‑stained hand, a half‑mask, and a diary that wants to document the absurdity of a school that pretends its rituals are serious. The only thing that makes me shiver is the sound of a cat’s purr in a hallway where blood emojis are being dropped like confetti.*  

I turned back toward the old library’s exit, the flashlight beam dimming as I left the dim room. The hallway beyond was quieter, the lockers standing like sentinels, each with a faint red line or no line at all. I could feel the tension of the day’s events pulling at me, a tug between curiosity and the fear that this is a prank that could spiral into a real lockdown, a real blood‑donation scandal, or a genuine night‑time drama that might land me in the headmaster’s office.  

Before I could step out onto the open corridor, the door behind me creaked open. A figure slipped through, a silhouette of a teen with a satchel, eyes darting, whispering, “You’re going to need a *real* cat, Jammie. The Crimson Club won’t let you cheat.” Zara’s voice cut through the hallway’s echo, the words sharp as a knife. “I’ve already sent you the Discord link. If you’re serious, you’ll find a *real* cat – Molly – in the chapel. She’ll be the *blood* that makes the night… *real*.”  

She vanished as quickly as she appeared, the echo of her voice lingering like a warning. I paused, notebook clutched to my chest, the red button digging into my palm. The rain hammered the windows with a steady rhythm, each drop sounding like a tiny drumbeat that matched the club’s upcoming “blood‑night” ceremony.  

My mind raced through possibilities: a staged charity drive, a viral TikTok prank, a genuine threat that could turn the school into a mock‑cult. The caretaker’s mutter about “bloody typical” still rang in my ears, and the janitor’s empty mop bucket suggested someone had already begun cleaning up a mess that hadn’t even happened.  

I slipped the rag‑stained pocket‑notebook into my blazer, the ink already smudging the edges, and felt the weight of my own words press against my chest. I closed the door behind me, the old wooden latch clicking, and stepped into the hallway where the light from the corridors seemed softer, as if the building itself was trying to dim the drama.  

*End of entry.*  

The red button on my jacket glowed faintly, a small promise that tonight would be more than a usual night of study. I could hear the faint thud of a cat’s paw in the distance, Molly perhaps already making her way toward the chapel, and I knew I was either about to become a willing participant in a darkly comic ritual, or I’d be the one who walks away with a scar that never quite heals. Either way, the blood emojis on my phone had given me a map—three drops, one eye, a line of red on a locker, and a smile that felt both ridiculous and sincere.  

I turned my head toward the chapel’s silhouette, the rain‑slick stone glistening in the early morning light, and whispered to the empty hallway, “I’ll write this down, and I’ll see where the blood leads.”  

The day stretched ahead, heavy with the smell of stale tea and the distant echo of a school’s announcement about a charity drive. I felt a little more like a detective and a little more like a prankster, and the line between the two seemed as thin as the red lipstick stain on my hand.  

I closed the diary, placed it on the desk, and stepped out of the library, ready to meet the Crimson Club—whether it wanted to be a satire, a charity, or a genuine horror, I was determined to keep my voice alive, ink-stained, and ready for the night to come.  

*End of entry.*

**Chapter 4 – Tunnel & Tails**  

02:18 am – I’m already half‑asleep when the alarm in my head rings louder than the school’s fire alarm. The phone on the night‑stand shows a single 👀 emoji, perched like a curious spider on the screen. I blink, rub the red‑lipstick stain off my knuckle, and pull the notebook from the drawer. The page is already half‑filled with the night’s events, but tonight I need a new entry.  

> **Entry 4/8 – “Tunnel & Tails”**  
> I’m back in the chapel after the “ghost‑hunter” meeting. The old library’s hidden passage is a thing of legend. I heard the caretaker mutter “bloody typical” when I asked about the red smear on locker 23. He’s right; there’s a stone tunnel underneath the chapel, sealed for years. I’ve just found it, and something—Molly, the stray cat—has a fresh wound on her paw. The Crimson Club thinks it’s a prop. I’m not sure.  

The rain outside the windows has softened into a steady drizzle, the water making the old stained‑glass windows look like a set of faded emojis. I sit on the cold stone floor, the notebook’s leather cover slick with a few drops that have seeped through the cracked floorboards. My breath fogs the air in front of me, a thin veil that seems to part as if a secret is about to be revealed.

The moment I turn the flashlight off and listen, the faint sound of a scurrying cat reaches me—a low, guttural mew that vibrates through the hollowed stone. I follow it, the beam of my flashlight cutting a narrow slice through the darkness. The corridor beneath the chapel is narrower than the hallway above, its walls lined with weathered bricks that have seen centuries of prayer and, now, teen conspiracies.  

A thin crack in the floorboard of the chapel’s east wing is the entrance I’m looking for. It’s hidden behind a loose piece of marble that looks like a decorative plaque but, when I shift it, reveals a narrow opening. I crouch, the flashlight trembling, and slide my shoulder into the tunnel. The air is cooler, a chill that makes the tiny hairs on my arms stand up, and the scent of damp stone mingles with something sweeter—old incense, faint as a memory.  

Inside, the tunnel is roughly six feet wide, the ceiling low enough that I have to duck my head. The walls are covered in graffiti: a sketch of a skull, a line of blood‑drop emojis, and a crude drawing of a cat with a red paw. The graffiti isn’t recent—it looks like someone has been marking this place for years, the ink darkened by time and the occasional spill of something sticky.  

The caretaker’s words echo in my head: “Bloody typical.” I realise the tunnel was used in the 1970s for a “blood‑donation drive” after a flu epidemic—students would line up with needles, the school’s chaplain would bless the blood, and the hallway would be lit with candles. That history feels like a joke in itself, a darkly comic footnote that now has a new protagonist: me, a scholarship boy with a half‑mask, a red‑stained pocket‑watch, and a cat named Molly who seems to have stumbled onto this relic.

I move deeper, the flashlight casting long shadows that dance against the graffiti. The tunnel ends in a small alcove, a room no larger than a broom closet, lit by a single oil lamp that flickers weakly. On the floor lies a wooden crate, its lid half‑open, spilling ketchup, fake‑blood packets, and a handful of red‑blotted bandages. The crate’s label reads, in a hand‑scribbled font, “Props – Blood Night”.  

My heart hammers a rhythm that matches the ticking of my pocket‑watch. I’m reminded of the Crimson Club’s staged charity drive—Lord Crimson’s line about “pouring sanguine tears upon the stone”. The idea that they’d use ketchup as a substitute for real blood, that they’d make a spectacle of something sacred for the sake of a viral TikTok, feels both absurd and chilling. I glance at the oil lamp, its flame licking the ceiling, and see a tiny speck of ash that looks like a tiny red droplet—coincidence? The caretaker’s mutter, “bloody typical”, seems to have come true.

At the far end of the alcove, a faint rustle announces the presence of the cat. Molly, the ginger stray with a scar on her right ear, is perched on a pile of dusty textbooks, her tail flicking in the dim light. She stares at me, eyes amber, the meow barely audible. I pull the rag‑stained notebook from my blazer pocket, the ink smudged on the corner from the locker’s strawberry jam. I lift the rag, the stain of the jam now a darker, more realistic smear of red against the light.

“Molly,” I whisper, the word tasting like a joke, “you’ve got a wound.” I notice a thin line of fresh blood on her paw—tiny, but real, a puncture from a broken bottle left outside the chapel after the lunch rush. The cat’s wound is small, a shallow cut that has already begun to clot. I crouch, hands trembling, and gently press a bandage from the crate onto the wound. The ketchup splotches on the bandage make it look like a parody of first aid, a gag that would make a teen audience laugh, but now it feels too real.

Lord Crimson’s voice drifts into my memory, the line he delivered in the chapel’s rehearsal: “If you see this, you’re in.” The crate, the graffiti, the cat’s wound—these are the symbols he left behind. I’m holding them in my hands, and the absurdity of the moment is overwhelming. I think of Zara’s message: “Don’t touch it, I’m not going to be the one cleaning up a mess of ketchup and cat fur.” Yet here I am, the only one willing to tend the cat.

I write a quick line in the notebook, the ink now darkened by the ketchup.  

> “The cat’s paw bleeds. The crate is full of ketchup and fake‑blood. The tunnel smells of old prayers and a hint of the chapel’s incense. I’m not sure whether the Crimson Club wants a *prop* cat or a *real* one. Either way, I’m stuck in a secret tunnel with a half‑mask and a pocket‑watch that ticks louder than the school’s alarm.”  

The door to the tunnel is sealed behind me, a metal latch that clicks when I pull it. I step back into the chapel, the echo of my footsteps sounding like a drumbeat for some unseen ritual. The chapel’s stone floor is still wet from the rain seeping through the cracks, a thin sheen of water catching the occasional flash of red from the cracked wall’s graffiti. The ancient organ sits silent, its pipes dusted, waiting for a future that might never come.

I hear a distant shuffle in the hallway—Zara’s voice, low and hurried, as if she’s trying to sneak a message past the night‑watch.  

> **Zara (text):** “I’m on the roof. The tunnel’s at the west wing. There’s a panel you can pry open with a screwdriver. I’ll meet you there in ten minutes. Bring the cat.”  

I glance at the clock on the wall; it reads 02:30, the minute hand ticking like the pocket‑watch. I realise I have a choice: I can retreat, keep the diary as a personal record, and leave the Crimson Club’s secrets to their own theatrical chaos. Or I can follow Zara, get the cat to the “Blood Night” rehearsal, and become a part of whatever absurd drama they intend to stage. The school’s rules, Mrs. Finch’s stern warning about “no extra drama after curfew,” weigh against the lure of being part of something that feels both dangerous and gloriously ridiculous.

I slip the notebook back into my pocket, the red‑stained rag now tangled with the ketchup bandage. My hand brushes the pocket‑watch, its ticking louder than the rain’s patter. I think of the caretaker’s mutter again, “bloody typical.” It’s a phrase that feels like a warning and a joke rolled into one. The cat’s soft purr, the faint smell of old incense, the faint glow of the oil lamp—these are the sensory anchors that make the night feel lived, not staged.

Lord Crimson’s presence lingers in the shadows, though I haven’t seen him again. I can picture his cane clicking against the stone, his voice saying, “You’ll write, Jammie. You’ll be a witness.” The idea that the Crimson Club wants a diary as a “real‑blood” companion is oddly fitting. My own diary, with its ink‑stained pages and half‑finished jokes, is already the witness. I decide to keep going, not because I’m brave, but because the absurdity of this moment feels like a badge I can’t resist.

I climb the narrow stairs that lead from the chapel to the west wing, each step creaking as if the building itself is an old, tired performer. The hallway beyond is dimly lit by a single fluorescent bulb, its hum matching the soft ticking of my watch. A locker at the far end bears the same scar‑red paw print as the graffiti—an accidental echo of Molly’s wound. I pause, press my hand against the locker, and feel a faint vibration, like the echo of a heartbeat.

The locker’s hinge squeaks, and a faint whisper of wind slips through the crack, carrying a scent of fresh paint and something metallic. I open it, revealing a set of screwdrivers, a small toolbox, and a note scrawled on the inside: “For the tunnel. Use wisely.” The note is in Zara’s handwriting, her font sharp, her initials at the bottom. She’s already prepared for the next step, a plan that feels both meticulously organized and wildly improvisational.

I lock the note in my mind, the image of Zara perched on the roof, the cat hissing, the Crimson Club’s cryptic “If you see this, you’re in” echoing in my head. The cat’s eyes lock onto mine, a silent promise that whatever we do, it will be witnessed. I pull a screwdriver from the locker, the metal cold against my fingertips, and head toward the west wing’s hidden panel—a narrow wooden door that looks like a maintenance hatch, barely noticed by the other students.

The panel’s hinges are rusty, the wood groaning as I pry it open. A thin shaft of light streams into the tunnel, illuminating the ketchup‑stained floor and the graffiti. I hear a faint rustle, a soft meow, and see Molly slip through the gap, her paw still bearing a faint crimson line. She looks at me, tail flicking, and then disappears into the darkness beyond the panel. I follow, the flashlight’s beam cutting a thin path, the sound of my own breathing amplified in the stone.

At the end of the tunnel, there’s a small alcove that opens onto the school’s rooftop, a flat expanse of gravel, low‑rising walls, and the faint outline of the chapel’s spire against a cloudy night sky. Zara stands there, a hoodie pulled over her shoulders, a half‑mask perched on her cheek, a look of mischief mixed with determination. Her eyes meet mine, and she smiles—a thin line that cuts across her cheek.  

> **Zara:** “You’re late. Molly’s already got a fresh bandage, and the Crimson Club’s waiting. We’ve got a schedule: fake blood at 22:00, ketchup for the ‘Blood Night’, and a real donation drive at 18:00. You’re on diary duty. Make sure you write something worth reading.”  

She tosses a crumpled piece of paper onto the ground—a set of instructions for the “Blood Night” ceremony. I pick it up, the ink smudged by rain, the words barely legible:  

1. **Set up “blood‑tea” station** – red tea made with beet juice, served in chipped cups.  
2. **Place fake blood** – use food‑grade red gelatin on the altar.  
3. **Invite Molly** – as the “prop cat”.  

The plan feels like a parody of charity, a satire of teenage drama, but the reality of a real cat with a real wound makes it feel too close to a horror. I glance at the cat’s paw, a thin line of blood now crusted, and feel a pang of responsibility. I’m not a vampire, I’m not a detective, but I’m stuck in a secret tunnel with a half‑mask, a pocket‑watch that ticks louder than the school’s fire alarm, and a cat that looks like it’s ready to star in a horror‑comedy.  

I write a quick line in the notebook, the ink bleeding through the red‑stained rag, the words forming like a trail of blood:  

> “The tunnel beneath the chapel is a relic of old blood‑drives, a playground for the Crimson Club’s dark jokes. Molly’s paw is real, the ketchup is fake, the night is a stage where the line between satire and danger blurs. I’m not sure where I’m headed, but I’ve found a hidden door and a half‑masked friend. The emojis on my phone were a map, and now the map is a tunnel.”  

The rain continues to drum against the stone, each drop a metronome for the night’s upcoming “Blood Night”. The school’s intercom crackles softly, announcing a “fire drill at 22:00” – an ironic timing, a reminder that the night is not just about blood, but also about the routine of school life that never stops.  

I tuck the notebook back into my blazer pocket, the pocket‑watch now a cold weight against my chest. The tunnel’s air feels thicker now, as if the walls are whispering. I hear the faint echo of Lord Crimson’s line, “You’ll write, Jammie. You’ll be a witness,” reverberating like a promise. I think of the caretaker’s mutter, “bloody typical,” and realize that the night is indeed typical—full of secrets, half‑concealed rituals, and a cat that’s more than a prop.  

I step back into the chapel, the stone floor still slick, the light from the oil lamp flickering. The chapel’s stone walls, once a solemn place of worship, now feel like a stage set for a dark comedy. I glance at the red button on my jacket, the tiny imprint now a reminder that I’ve officially joined the Crimson Club. I haven’t decided whether that means I’m a participant or a victim, but I know that the next chapter will involve more ketchup, more jokes, and maybe, just maybe, a genuine blood‑donation drive that could make the night feel both absurd and worthwhile.  

I close the diary entry, press the red button, and feel the faint click of the latch behind me. The tunnel’s echo fades, but the cat’s soft purr lingers in the hallway, a reminder that even the darkest jokes have a living heartbeat.  

*End of entry.*  

The night is still thick with rain, the school’s bells echo faintly, and I’m left with a notebook that smells of ink, ketchup, and the faint metallic tang of old stone. I don’t know where the story will go, but I do know that the next step will be to bring Molly to the chapel, to watch the Crimson Club’s “Blood Night” unfold, and to keep writing, because the only thing that can make a night like this bearable is a diary full of jokes, half‑serious thoughts, and a cat whose paw still drips.  

The tunnel beneath the chapel is a reminder that the school is older than any of us, that its walls have heard secrets, that its stone can hide a blood‑drive, a prank, or a charity. The Crimson Club wants to paint the night with blood emojis, but they’ve found a real cat, a real wound, and a real tunnel. I’m stuck in the middle, a scholarship boy with a half‑mask, a pocket‑watch that ticks louder than the school’s alarm, and a diary that will document whatever absurdity awaits.  

I step back out into the hallway, the rain now a steady drum on the roof, and hear the distant sound of the school’s fire alarm—only this time, it feels like a cue for the next scene. I tuck my notebook into my bag, the red‑stained rag still clinging to my sleeve, and make my way toward the rooftop, where Zara waits with a half‑smile, ready to set the stage for a night that will be both bloody and comedic.  

*End of entry.*

> **Entry 5/8 – “Midnight Blood‑Night”**

The Crimson Club has long been a mock‑ritual for those who feel the school’s bureaucracy is as dull as a school lunch. Tonight we shall prove that drama can be served with a side of ketchup.”  

He smiles, a thin line that stretches across his cheek, and gestures toward the altar. The altar is a modest stone slab, topped with a cracked wooden table that has been used for decades for school ceremonies. He pulls a small bag from his jacket, its contents spilling out in a cascade of red gelatin, beet‑juice tea bags, and a handful of ketchup packets. The gelatin is a cheap stage‑blood substitute, the kind you find in a Halloween prop shop; the beet juice is meant to colour the tea, giving it a “blood‑tea” hue; the ketchup will be used for the “spilling” effect, a nod to the club’s obsession with making the mundane look morbid.  

He looks at me, the half‑mask I wore during the ghost‑hunter entrance still half‑hanging from my jaw, and says, “Jammie, you’ve been chosen as the witness. You’ll keep the diary, you’ll note the cadence of each drop, and you’ll, dare I say, keep your mind sharp enough to write a decent review. This is the night the Crimson Club will finally have a proper audience.”  

I swallow, the taste of stale tea and cheap lip‑stick lingering on my tongue, and nod. My notebook feels like a weapon, its pages ready to capture the absurdity before the school’s alarm system catches us. Zara arrives a moment later, her hoodie pulled over her shoulders, a half‑mask perched on her cheek, a grin that says “I’m here for the tech”. She pulls a small tablet from her bag, tapping a few buttons, and the chapel’s PA system crackles to life.  

> “Attention, all students,” a voice, artificially deepened, says, “the Crimson Club will be hosting a Blood Night charity event tonight at 22:00 in the chapel. All proceeds will go to the local blood‑bank. Bring your curiosity and your… *anonymity*.”  

The announcement is broadcast over the intercom, a perfect blend of bureaucratic formality and teenage meme culture. It feels like a prank, but it also feels like a genuine charity drive, which is exactly what the club wants: a mock‑ritual that can be re‑interpreted as a real donation drive, thereby sidestepping any suspicion of actual malice.  

Molly, the stray cat, pads into the chapel through the cracked doorway, her scarred ear glinting in the weak light. She pauses at the altar, tail flicking, and then leans against the stone pillar, her eyes locked onto Lord Crimson’s cane. She looks like she’s auditioning for a role in a horror‑comedy, but the reality of her wounded paw—still a fresh line of blood that has begun to clot—makes me feel a pang of guilt. I remember the bandage I slipped onto her paw earlier; it was a rag‑stained piece of ketchup, a parody of first‑aid that now seems more sincere. I glance at the pocket‑watch on my wrist, its ticking louder than the rain outside, and I think of the caretaker’s mutter, “bloody typical”. The cat’s presence feels like a bridge between the club’s theatrical blood‑play and a genuine animal in need.

Lord Crimson gestures for the “blood‑tea” station. Zara quickly pulls out a kettle from the side pantry, fills it with water, and drops a beet‑juice packet into the boiling liquid. The colour deepens, turning the tea a shade of rusty red. She whispers, “If we’re doing a blood‑tea toast, we need a proper setting—chipped teacups, a cracked tablecloth, and a fake‑blood spread on the floor.” She lays a tattered tablecloth over the altar, the red stains already set, and I notice the faint outline of the three blood emojis drawn in ketchup on the cloth, as if someone has already marked the stage for a perfect “scene”.  

The club members—Lord Crimson, a few senior boys with half‑masked faces, and a handful of younger students—begin to arrange the props. They stack the red gelatin packets in neat rows, the ketchup bottles in a corner, and the beet‑juice tea in a copper pot that steams faintly. The school’s caretaker, Mr. Hargrave, peeks in from the doorway, his eyes narrowing, a faint smile forming on his lips. He mutters, “Bloody typical,” and then steps back, leaving a small brass key on the altar that looks like it could open the tunnel beneath the chapel.  

I watch the rehearsal, the flashlight beam moving like a slow camera pan, capturing each absurd detail. Lord Crimson delivers a flamboyant speech, pulling quotes from Macbeth and Hamlet, mixing them with modern slang: “We shall *spill* our secrets, not our blood—though the latter is certainly more dramatic.” He lifts his cane, points it at the ceiling, and shouts, “Let the blood run, let the laughter follow!” The club members respond with exaggerated gasps, some clutching their own pockets, as if the imagined blood might seep through their clothing.  

Zara slides a tablet onto the altar, showing a slide of the club’s Discord channel, the emojis flickering like a neon sign. She overlays the channel’s text onto the altar’s stone, making the emojis appear as if they’re etched into the stone. The red emoji, the 👀, the skull, each glows faintly, a reminder that the club’s digital language has invaded the physical world. She whispers, “If anyone asks, we’ll say it’s a charity fundraiser. The blood‑donation drive is real; the *blood* is fake.”  

Molly’s purr becomes louder, a low‑frequency warning that seems to vibrate through the stone floor. I realize that the cat’s presence is not merely a prop but a living reminder that the night’s drama can cross into real pain. The wound on her paw is still there, a thin line of blood that has begun to crust. I glance at my notebook, the ink now smeared with ketchup, and write a quick line:  

> “The cat’s paw still bleeds; the club’s props are ketchup‑stained. I’m a witness, a half‑mask, a pocket‑watch that ticks louder than the chapel’s organ. The Crimson Club wants a satire that looks like charity, and the only thing that might stop the performance is a real cat.”  

Lord Crimson’s final line is a rehearsed pause, a breath before he says, “Now, to the *real* blood‑donation drive. Let the donations flow, and let the ketchup stay where it belongs—on the floor, not on our hearts.” He looks at me, his eyes bright with theatrical fervour, and says, “Jammie, you’ll write the review. You’ll make sure the world knows we’re more than a prank.”  

I swallow the mixture of nerves and excitement. The rain has turned the chapel’s windows into a veil of water, and the night outside feels like an audience waiting for the curtain to rise. I feel the weight of the pocket‑watch, its ticking echoing in my ears, a reminder that time is limited. The headmaster, Mr. Finch, is supposed to be out on patrol, but his voice is heard over the PA, dry as a British weather report:  

> “If anyone is planning a ‘Blood Night’ that isn’t a charity event, please report it to the pastoral care office. This is a school, not a horror‑film set.”  

The comment is delivered with a smile, a thin line that suggests he’s amused by the absurdity but also wary of any genuine danger. He adds, “And remember, a proper tea is still the best refreshment after midnight.”  

Zara pulls a small speaker from her bag, humming a low bass line that mimics a heartbeat, and the club members begin a mock “blood‑donation” chant: “Give what you can, bleed what you want.” They pretend to pour the red gelatin onto the floor, the ketchup splashing in a way that looks like a real spill. The sound is absurdly loud in the echoing chapel, a discordant blend of stage directions and teenage rebellion.  

I stand at the edge of the scene, notebook clutched to my chest, flashlight still on, trying to capture every detail. I hear the faint rustle of the cat’s tail, the distant clang of the old organ’s pipes, and the faint buzz of the school’s fire alarm, which has been set to go off at 22:00 as a routine drill—ironically timed with the club’s “Blood Night”. The alarm’s echo will soon be a drumbeat for whatever chaos is about to unfold, but right now the only thing that feels like a threat is my own imagination, the thrill of being part of a secret society that might actually turn the chapel into a stage for something more than a prank.  

I think about the three blood emojis that arrived on my phone at midnight, the 👀 emoji that followed, the red‑stained rag I used to bandage Molly, the ketchup packets that now litter the floor like tiny splashes of absurdity. I think about the caretaker’s mutter, “bloody typical”, and how it feels less like a warning now and more like a shared joke. The Crimson Club’s plan to use the school’s charity drive as cover feels like a clever satire, but the real risk is that someone might take it seriously—perhaps a teacher, perhaps a student who misreads the joke as a threat.  

Lord Crimson leans close to me, whispering, “Remember, Jammie, the truth is always redder than the fake. Write it down, and you’ll make sure the night stays a story, not a nightmare.” His voice is low, almost conspiratorial, and I feel a sudden surge of adrenaline. I nod, the notebook’s pages now feeling like a shield against any unwanted prying eyes.  

Zara steps back, eyes sparkling with tech‑savvy confidence, and says, “If anyone asks for proof, we’ve got the Discord screenshots, the PA announcement, and the cat’s paw. The world will think we’re just a satire, and the blood‑bank will get a few extra donations—maybe even enough for a proper tea.”  

The rain intensifies, droplets hammering the stone roof like a drumroll for the upcoming “show”. I glance at the clock on the chapel wall; the minute hand ticks toward 22:00, the hour hand hovering on the “12”. The night feels like a stage, the chapel a set, and the characters—Jammie the reluctant witness, Zara the tech‑witch, Lord Crimson the flamboyant director, Molly the prop cat—are all positioned for a moment that could either dissolve into laughter or spiral into something more unsettling.  

I write a final line in my diary, the ink smearing a little as the ketchup drips onto the page:  

> “Midnight Blood‑Night is set. The Crimson Club rehearses on the altar, a cat watches, a fake blood‑donation drive is announced, and my pocket‑watch ticks louder than the school’s alarm. I’m not sure if I’m part of a satire or a genuine threat, but I’ll write it down, because the only thing that can make a night like this bearable is a notebook full of jokes, half‑serious thoughts, and a cat whose paw still drips.”  

The flashlight’s beam flickers, the candlelight from the altar sputters, and the faint smell of beet juice and ketchup mingles with the old incense. The night outside is a steady drum, and inside the chapel the silence is broken only by the occasional hiss of the fake blood gelatin hardening, the click of the pocket‑watch, and the low purr of a cat that may or may not be a prop. I close the diary, tuck the red button into my jacket pocket, and step back toward the hallway, the rain still pounding against the glass, the emoji chain lingering in my mind like a reminder that the world can be both a joke and a warning.  

I think about the next steps: the real Blood Night ceremony, the potential lock‑down, the possible viral TikTok reaction, the charity blood‑bank’s response, and the possibility that Mr Finch will walk in with a dead‑pan comment about tea. I know I’ll have to keep watching, keep writing, keep the pocket‑watch ticking, and perhaps, one day, the club’s satirical drama will bleed into something more—whether a harmless prank or a genuine scar on the school’s history.  

For now, the night is a stage, and I am the reluctant playwright, holding a notebook that smells of ink, ketchup, and the faint metallic tang of old stone. The three blood emojis on my phone have become a series of tiny invitations, each one a red line that connects me to a world of half‑masks, half‑truths, and a cat that may just be more than a prop.  

*End of entry.*  

The rain continues, the chapel’s stone walls echo with whispers, and the darkness outside feels like a curtain waiting to rise. I close the diary, the red button still pressed against my chest, and think about the next midnight text—perhaps it will be another emoji, perhaps a single word, perhaps a new mystery. Whatever it is, I’ll be ready, notebook in hand, flashlight at the ready, and a half‑mask that refuses to come off.  

*End of entry.*

**Chapter 6 – Breach & Ketchup**  

00:32 am – the chapel’s old organ emits a sudden, jerky wobble, as if the wind has slipped a few notes into its pipes. The scent of beet‑juice tea hangs heavy in the air, mingling with the faint tang of ketchup that has already seeped into the cracks of the stone floor. I stare at the red button on my jacket, the tiny imprint still warm against my skin, and feel the pocket‑watch tick louder than ever – its second hand sweeping over the 00:30 mark like a metronome for a disaster that hasn’t yet happened.  

> **Entry 6/8 – “Breach & Ketchup”**  
> The headmaster’s alarm sounds at 22:00, but the Crimson Club’s “Blood Night” rehearsal is already a mess. Police arrive, think the ketchup spill is a cult ritual. Lord Crimson swears by Shakespeare, Zara hacks the PA, Molly the cat darts through the chaos, leaving a fresh paw‑print in the ketchup. I’m stuck in the middle, diary in hand, trying not to get drenched in sauce or arrested for a prank that might actually be a charity drive.  

The chapel’s fire alarm blares a sharp, metallic clang that reverberates off the vaulted ceiling. The sound is followed by the muffled thud of footsteps – a mix of shuffling students, the heavy tread of a school security guard, and the distant rumble of a police siren turning the night into a full‑blown lock‑down. The intercom crackles, a voice I recognize instantly as Mr Finch’s, dry as a British weather forecast:  

> “All students, please remain calm. The chapel is under investigation for… a potential disturbance. This is a routine lockdown, not a theatrical performance. Stay in your dormitory rooms until further notice.”  

Lord Crimson’s flamboyant proclamation collapses under the sudden onslaught of authority. He clutches his skull‑engraved cane, the one he’s used to tap rhythm into his speeches, and shouts, “No! This is not a riot! This is satire!” His voice is drowned out by the sirens, which wail a three‑note motif that feels more like a horror movie cue than a police broadcast.  

I’m halfway to the stairwell when Zara bursts in, her hoodie pulled over her shoulders, a half‑mask perched on her cheek. She looks wild-eyed, but her smile is razor‑sharp. “We’ve got the PA hacked, Jammie,” she whispers, “the ‘Blood Night’ announcement is still live. They’re hearing a charity drive, not a cult. Let’s keep the ketchup flowing – it’ll look like a real ‘spill’ to the cops.”  

She pulls a small tablet from her bag, presses a button, and the chapel speakers blast a distorted version of the original announcement, now layered with a police‑style “All clear” tone. The echo of the fake blood‑donation drive is now intertwined with the sound of a siren, making the whole scene feel like a mash‑up of a school assembly and a BBC crime drama. The police officers, three of them – a constable in a crisp uniform, a senior sergeant with a clipboard, and a constable who keeps muttering “Bloody hell, this is insane” under his breath – enter the chapel through the side door, their flashlights cutting through the red‑tinged haze.  

“What the hell is going on?” the constable barks, his voice rough as a gravel road. “We have reports of a ‘blood‑spill’ at the chapel. Is this a hoax?”  

Lord Crimson steps forward, his burgundy scarf fluttering, and bows dramatically. “Good evening, gentlemen. I am Lord Crimson, founder of the Crimson Club, a satirical secret society dedicated to… *exaggerating* the ordinary. We are hosting a charity blood‑donation drive. The ketchup is… well, it’s a prop. We apologize for any confusion.”  

The sergeant glances at the floor, sees a sea of ketchup spreading like a crimson river across the stone tiles, and a faint red smear near the altar. He raises an eyebrow, then says, “If this is a prank, you’ve done a spectacular one. If it’s real, you’re going to need a proper blood‑bank on site. We’ll have to verify.”  

Meanwhile, Molly the ginger stray cat darts between the officers’ feet, her injured paw leaving a fresh, vivid line of blood that has begun to clot. She stops, looks up at the constable, then darts back into the shadows, her eyes flickering like tiny red emojis on a black screen. The constable mutters, “Bloody hell, a cat with a wound in the middle of a ketchup spill. This is… insane.”  

I’m pressed against the wall, notebook clutched to my chest, flashlight beam trembling in my hand. My red‑stained rag, the one I used to bandage Molly’s paw, is now smeared across the floor, a tiny patch of real blood glinting in the flashlight’s harsh light. I feel the weight of the pocket‑watch as it ticks louder than the police’s radios. The rain outside the chapel windows has turned the stone into a mirror, reflecting the police lights in a dizzying pattern that looks like a flickering emoji chain.  

Zara pulls a roll of duct tape from her bag, hands it to the constable, and says, “We’ll seal the ketchup leak, Officer. It’s not real blood.” The constable snorts, “If you’re trying to keep the blood‑bank donation legit, you’ve done a great job.”  

Lord Crimson, ever theatrical, lifts his cane, points it at the ceiling, and declares, “Ladies and gentlemen, let us perform the *final act* – the ‘blood‑tea’ toast!” He reaches for a chipped teacup from the side table, the one we prepared for the “Blood Night” ceremony, and pours the beet‑juice tea into it. The liquid glows an unsettling crimson, like a miniature river of fake blood. He raises the cup, his voice echoing, “To the Crimson Club, to satire, and to the un‑ending rain that keeps the school’s stone walls wet!”  

A sudden shudder in the floor announces the arrival of the school’s maintenance crew, the old boiler room door swinging open with a groan. The caretaker, Mr Hargrave, steps in, his eyes scanning the mess, a faint smile playing on his lips. “Bloody typical,” he mutters, “the night always ends with a splash of sauce.” He pulls a broom, sweeps the ketchup into a bucket, and the red liquid clings to his palms, a reminder that the club’s “prop” has become a literal mess.  

The sergeant, noticing the bucket, leans in, sniffing. “Is that… ketchup? That’s… surprisingly realistic.” He chuckles, a low, nervous laugh that feels like a tiny drumbeat in the chaos. “Alright, boys, we’ll give you a pass for the ‘artistic expression.’ Just make sure the real blood‑donation drive goes through the proper channels tomorrow.”  

Zara nods, her eyes flashing with the same fire that drove her to hack the PA. “The donation drive is already listed on the school’s charity board. We’ll just add a note that the ‘blood‑night’ was a mock‑up for publicity.” She pulls out a small flyer, the paper crisp, printed with the Crimson Club logo and the words “Charity Blood‑Donation – 18:00 – Chapel.”  

I glance at the flyer, the ink smudged with ketchup on the corner, and think about the three blood‑drop emojis that started this whole mess. They’ve become a chain reaction, a cascade of absurdity that has turned a simple midnight text into a full‑blown lock‑down, a police investigation, and a culinary disaster. I feel the urge to write a note in my diary, but my hand trembles, the flashlight’s beam flickering over my notebook pages.  

> “The chapel is a stage, the police are the audience, and the ketchup is the blood. The Crimson Club’s satire has turned into a ‘real’ drama, and the school’s alarm system is now a drumbeat for a disaster that could have been avoided if someone had just asked, ‘Why are we pouring ketchup?’”  

I hear a distant voice from the hallway, faint but clear: “Jammie, if you’re still there, meet me at the roof. The lock‑down’s over, but the Crimson Club’s meeting is still on.” It’s Zara, her half‑mask glinting under the police lights. She tosses a crumpled piece of paper onto the floor – a schedule for tomorrow’s “real” blood‑donation drive, complete with a list of volunteers, the school’s blood‑bank contact, and a note: “Bring a cat for good luck.”  

I pocket the paper, the ketchup still staining my fingers, and glance at the red button on my jacket. It feels like a badge, a mark that says I’m now officially part of the Crimson Club, whether I like it or not. I think about Molly’s paw, the fresh wound that still bears a faint crimson line. The cat’s presence reminds me that even a staged “blood‑night” can have real consequences – a bleeding animal, a frightened student, a lock‑down that could have been a simple prank.  

Lord Crimson steps closer, his voice now a low whisper, as if the police officers have turned the chapel into a courtroom. “Remember, Jammie, the truth is always… *redder* than the fake. The charity drive will go ahead, the ketchup will be cleaned, and the *real* blood will be donated.” He looks at the constable, then at me, and says, “You have a choice: write the scandal, or write the redemption.”  

The sergeant nods, his eyes scanning the hallway for any more “blood‑drops.” “We’ll close the investigation. If you’re serious about the donation, you’ll need a proper blood‑bank, not a ketchup spill. And if you’re just playing… keep the pranks to after‑school hours.”  

I hear a faint rustle behind a pew, and out of the shadows steps a figure – a senior boy from the robotics club, his face half‑masked, holding a small USB stick. He whispers, “The Crimson Club’s Discord server is being monitored. If you want to keep the secret, we need to wipe the logs. Zara, you’ll have to delete the invite code.”  

Zara nods, pulling her hoodie tighter, and says, “I’ll do it. The police won’t find anything if the server’s gone. And the charity drive will go as planned – a genuine blood‑donation, not a staged spill.”  

The constable’s radio crackles, a voice announcing, “All clear. Students may return to dormitories. Lockdown lifted.” The police officers file out, their footsteps echoing down the narrow hallway, their eyes lingering on the ketchup‑stained floor, the cat’s fresh paw print, and the half‑mask half‑masked students. The chapel’s alarm clicks off, the organ’s pipes settling back into silence.  

I’m left standing in a puddle of ketchup, the smell of fake blood now competing with the scent of stale incense and the lingering sweetness of beet‑juice tea. The rain outside continues its steady drum, each drop hitting the chapel’s glass like a tiny, impatient emoji. I pull my notebook close, the red button still digging into my chest, and write a final line:  

> “The lock‑down turned the chapel into a crime scene, the police into confused tourists, and the ketchup into a ‘blood’ prop that almost got us arrested. The Crimson Club’s satire has become a mess of real and fake, but the charity blood‑donation drive is still on the schedule. I’m not sure whether I’m a witness or a participant, but I’ll keep the diary, because the only thing that can make a night like this bearable is a notebook full of jokes, half‑serious thoughts, and a cat whose paw still drips.”  

I hear a faint meow from the doorway, Molly the cat slipping back into the hallway, her paw now a faint line of blood against the wet floor. She pauses, looks at me, and then darts toward the stairs, disappearing into the darkness. I glance at the police officer who stayed a moment longer, his eyes flickering over the red button on my jacket, and I think about the headmaster’s comment earlier: “If you’re looking for proper drama, try a formal dinner. This is… well, a mess.”  

Lord Crimson steps back, his cane tapping a rhythm, and says, “Tomorrow, we’ll have a proper blood‑donation drive. No ketchup, no cat, just… real blood. And we’ll keep the jokes for after‑curfew.” He smiles, the same thin line that runs across his cheek, and I feel a strange relief. The chaos has given me a story, a reason to write, and a sense that the Crimson Club’s absurdity might have a heart of charity after all.  

I tuck the red button deeper into my pocket, the ketchup still clinging to my fingertips, and head toward the stairwell, the rain hammering the windows like a drumroll for the next act. The night outside is a blanket of black, the chapel’s stone walls now drenched, the flashlight beam my only guide. As I descend, I hear the faint echo of the headmaster’s voice from the intercom, his tone dry and dead‑pan:  

> “Remember, gentlemen, a proper tea is still the best refreshment after midnight.”  

I smile, a half‑smile that feels both sarcastic and sincere. The lock‑down is over, the police are gone, and the Crimson Club’s “Blood Night” has turned into a messy rehearsal that will be cleaned up, but the memory of ketchup on stone, a cat’s fresh wound, and a half‑mask performance will stay with me. I close the diary, the ink still smudged with ketchup, and think about the next midnight text – perhaps it will be a single 👀 emoji, or maybe a different symbol entirely. Whatever it is, I’ll be ready, notebook in hand, flashlight at the ready, and a half‑mask that refuses to come off.  

*End of entry.*  

The night feels a little less terrifying now that the police have left, but the rain continues its steady rhythm, and the scent of ketchup lingers like a reminder that satire and seriousness can bleed into each other. I’m still not sure whether I’m part of a prank, a charity, or something in between, but I know one thing: the Crimson Club’s next meeting will be on the roof, and Molly will be there, watching, waiting. The red button on my jacket is now a badge of participation, and the pocket‑watch ticks louder than ever, counting down the seconds until the real blood‑donation drive begins tomorrow.  

I step out onto the wet hallway, the stones slick beneath my shoes, and hear the distant sound of a cat’s purr echoing off the stone walls. The night is still thick with rain, the chapel’s stone floors still stained, but the chaos has given me a story to write, a night to remember, and a reason to keep my diary open.  

*End of entry.*
